content:
The essence of estimation The generation of information that has the following properties
 quality (accuracy/reliability) higher than the raw measurements contains information not directly available in the measurements  [Multitarget-multisensor Tracking: Principles and Techniques : Yakkov Bar-Shalom Xiao-Rong Li]
   Mathematical view of state estimation.   
 No access to variables inside the first two blocks The only accessible variable are the measurements which are affected by noise.  The estimator Uses knowledge and processes the measurements to yield an estimate of a variable of interest(state or state uncertainty), while trying to make best use of the available data. In this process it is taking into account the:
 System dynamics Sensor Models Prior information & probabilistic nature of the uncertainties  Tracking Tracking is the processing of measurements or observations obtained from a target in order to maintain an estimate of its current state
The state of a target usually consists of:
 Kinematics - position, velocity, acceleration , turn rate, etc. expressed w.r.t a coordinate frame Feature components - target classificiation, spectral characteristics, radar cross-section etc.  Measurements or observations are noise-corrupted quantities which are related to the state of a target, such as
 Direct estimate of position (GPS) Range and azimuth(bearing) from the sensor (radar, cameras) Range rate (radar)  The observations are not raw data sequences but usually the output of a complex signal processing subsystem.
   Tracking system components.   
Coordinate systems Four coordinate systems are usually involved in tracking algorithms.
 Geodetic Local-tangential Local spherical Earth centered Earth-fixed (ECEF) coordinates  The transformations between the systems exist and are non-linear.
Geodetic to ECEF GPS measurements usually arrive in geodetic coordinates, where as tracking is usually performed in the local tangential plane. The transformation between these two requires an intermediate transformation from geodetic $(\phi,\lambda,h)$ to ECEF $(X,Y,Z)$ coordinates.
  Geodetic coordinate system   \[ \begin{align*} x = 3 \\ y = 5 \end{align*} \]
\[\begin{align*} X &=(N+h)cos\lambda cos\phi \\ Y &= (N+h)cos\lambda sin\phi \\ Z &= [N(1-e^2)+h] sin\lambda \end{align*}\]
ECEF to local-tangential plane (ENU) The local-tangential plane is the east-north-up rectangular coordinate system and it is determined by fitting a tangential plane to the surface of the earth at some reference point. The reference point is the origin of the frame. Such a system is convenient in the context of tracking nearby ships because the motion and measurement models reduce to two-dimensional problems.
  ECEF and ENU systems   Given a reference point in Geodetic $[\phi_0,\lambda_0]$ and the corresponding ECEF $[X_0,Y_0,Z_0]$ coordinates, then any other point's ECEF coordinates $[X,Y,Z]$ can be converted to local tangential plane coordinates $[x,y,z]$ using the following transformation
\[ \begin{bmatrix} x\\ y\\ z \end{bmatrix}= \mathcal{L}\left(\begin{bmatrix} X\\ Y\\ Z \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} X_0\\ Y_0\\ Z_0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \]
where $\mathcal{L}$ is a function of the reference point $[\phi_0,\lambda_0,h_0]$
\[ \mathcal{L} = \begin{bmatrix} -sin\phi_0 & cos\phi_0 & 0 \\ -sin\lambda_0cos\phi_0 & -sin\lambda_0sin\phi_0 & cos\lambda_0\\ cos\lambda_0 cos\phi_0 & cos\lambda_0 sin\phi_0 & sin\lambda_0 \end{bmatrix} \]
Body fixed frame (or local tangential own-ship frame) This frame is the local-tangential frame rotated around the z-axis so that the x-axis is aligned with the own-ships heading direction from the true north $h_{self_{true}}$. This frame in case of a non rolling-pitching vessel coincides with the body-fixed frame. This is also called the sea-keeping frame because the vessel is assumed to move along the direction of its average velocity. Since this system is rotating along the vessel's heading direction and so do the local sensors, it is convenient to use for tracking purposes.
   Body fixed sea-keeping and NED systems   src: [Kinematic Models for Manoeuvring and Seakeeping of Marine Vessels]
The transformation thus is:
\[\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{ok}= R_{z}\]
\[\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{\text{w.r.t. own-ship}} = R_{z}(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{self_{true}}) \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}_{\text{local tangential}} \]
where
\[ R_{z}(\theta)=\begin{bmatrix} cos\theta &-sin\theta &0\\ sin\theta &cos\theta &0 \\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \]
Purpose of using different coordinate systems Both the motion and the measurement model equations depend on the coordinate system of choice. Sensor measurements from a radar usually arrive in the local spherical system, where as the GPS measurements either for the own-ship or for other vessels arrive in the geodetic system. It is convenient to express a target's motion model w.r.t the local tangential body fixed frame, since the local sensors like the radar and the cameras are rigidly attached to the body-frame. This simplifies the measurement models for local sensors, nevertheless it is creating the necessity to simplify the global measurement sensors like the AIS.
Motion model When choosing a coordinate system for the target motion model, it is very convenient to assume the target's motion on the local-tangential plane with the origin placed on the own-ship(body fixed frame).
The motion models proposed in literature are numerous. Trying to keep the complexity low, linear motion models assume a constant velocity(CV) or constant acceleration (CA). These models have the advantage that the state transition equation is linear. Unfortunately they assume straight motions and are thus unable to model rotational motion. When one introduces the angular speed of the target around the z-axis, the resulting models are referred to as curvilinear models.
Use of the local tangential own-ship coordinate system Tracking objects using the body-fixed planar frame is a fair assumption at sea since a target in the vicinity of the own-ship in this case can be projected on the self-body fixed frame, and is thus a fair approximation as long as the distance between the tracked target and the own-ship is not large enough for the earth's curvature to induce approximation errors. The model's fidelity using the body-fixed frame is a topic of debate. In the context of sensor fusion and local situational awareness, the on-board sensors used on a ship operate within a range limit that should --- at least intuitively --- ratify the tangential plane approximation.
When it comes to situational awareness in a more global context, i.e. tracking target vessels across the Atlantic, one could argue that there is no sense in applying sensor fusion since the targets are observable solely through GPS measurements.
The above is just an assumption at this point.
State vector Assuming the target's motion on a plane, then two different state-space descriptions can be used depending on the expression of the velocity vector:
 Cartesian velocity model \(\mathbf{x} = \left[x,y,\dot{x}, \dot{y},\omega\right]^{T} \)
 Polar velocity model \(\mathbf{x} = \left[x,y,\upsilon, h,\omega\right]^{T} \)
  where
\[ \begin{align*} \upsilon &= \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} \\ h &= arctan\left(\frac{\dot{y}}{\dot{x}}\right) \\ \omega &= \dot{h} \end{align*} \]
Where:
 $(x,y)$ is the displacement relative to the local self-body fixed coordinate system
 $\upsilon$ is the target's linear velocity
 $h$ is the heading angle
 $\omega$ is the turn-rate of the target
  all expressed w.r.t the own-ship's body frame which has the x-axis aligned with the own-ship heading direction.
Continuous constant turn rate and velocity (CTRV) model Starting with a time continuous model
\[\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = f(\mathbf{x}(t)) + w(t)\]
then assuming that the linear velocity \(\upsilon\) and the turn rate \(\omega\) remain constant --- which is a fair assumption for a moving vessel --- the non-linear dynamics are
\[f = \begin{bmatrix}\upsilon cos(h) \\ \upsilon sin(h) \\ 0 \\ \omega \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\]
which is describing constant turn rate and velocity(CTRV)model.
Discretisation of the CTRV motion model Since the system is simulated in discrete time, one can integrate the motion model within a sample time \(T\) and obtain exactly
\[ \mathbf{x}(t+T) = \mathbf{x}(t) + \int_{t}^{t+T}(f(\mathbf{x}(\tau)+w(\tau) )d\tau \]
and hence the discrete update model by ignoring the noise is
\[ \mathbf{x}(t+T) = g\left(\mathbf{x}(t)\right) \]
where
\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \frac{2\upsilon}{\omega}sin(\omega T)cos(h + \frac{\omega T}{2})\\ -\frac{2\upsilon}{\omega}sin(\omega T)cos(h + \frac{\omega T}{2})\\ 0 \\ \omega T \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} , \omega \neq 0 \]
or
\[ g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} \upsilon T cos(h)\\ \upsilon T sin(h)\\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} , \omega = 0 \]
Observation models An observation model $z_k = h(\mathbf{x_k}) + w_k$ expresses the observations $z_k$ at time instances \(t_k\) given the state vector $\mathbf{x}$ . $w_k$ is assumed to be white gaussian noise with covariance matrix that depends on the specific sensor. The selection of the state vector $\mathbf{x}$ can lead to linear or nonlinear observation models.
Radar observation model The radar observation model includes calculating the distance and bearing from the own-ship to the target in polar form $z_{radar} = \begin{bmatrix} \rho, \theta \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ , given the target's position $\begin{bmatrix}x_t, y_t\end{bmatrix}^{T}$ w.r.t the sensor's coordinate frame. If the radar's frame is aligned to the vessel's frame, then the radar measurement model can be expressed as:
\[ z_{radar}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \rho \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \\ arctan(\frac{x}{y}) \end{bmatrix} + w_{radar} \]
where the measurement noise \(w\) is assumed to be white, zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $R_{radar} = \text{diag}\left[\sigma^2_{\rho}, \sigma^2_{\theta}\right] $
A reasonable sizing of the noise variances can be
 $\sigma^2_\rho = 10 \textit{m}$ $\sigma^2_\theta = 3 \frac{\pi}{180} [rad] $  GPS observation model The GPS observation model calculates the target's relative position \(\begin{bmatrix}x, y\end{bmatrix}^{T} \) w.r.t the own-ship tangential coordinate frame given
 The own-ship's geodetic coordinates $\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}^{T}$ The own-ships sea-keeping heading angle from true north $h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}$ The target's measured geodetic coordinates $\begin{bmatrix}\phi, \lambda\end{bmatrix}^{T}$ which is usually available at sea through the Automatic Identification System.  \[ z_{GPS}(\mathbf{x},\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0}, h_{0_{\textit{TN}}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}) = \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix}_{GPS} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix} + w_{GPS} \]
The fact that the geodetic coordinates and the heading to true north of the self-vehicle is not included in the state-space representation leads to a linear measurement system, as the measurements are pre-transformed into the local-tangential plane w.r.t the self-vehicles heading direction and the noise is added on the the post-transformed measurements.
The AIS measurements can be transformed into local-tangential vessel coordinates by using the aforementioned transformations.
\[ \begin{bmatrix} x \\\\ y \end{bmatrix}_{\text{local tangential}} = R_z(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}) \cdot F_1\left(F_2(\begin{bmatrix} \phi \\\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}),\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0 \\\\ \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \]
Where
 \(F_1\left( \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}_{ECEF},\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0 \\ \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}\right)\) is the transformation from ECEF to ENU, given the geodetic coordinates of the own-ship $\begin{bmatrix} \phi_0, \lambda_0 \end{bmatrix}^T$ as a reference point.
 $F_2\left(\begin{bmatrix} \phi \lambda \end{bmatrix}\right)$ is the transformation from geodetic to ECEF coordinates
 $R_z(\theta)$ is the transformation from ENU to sea-keeping frame.
  AIS observation model An alternative method to using the aforementioned GPS model is to leverage the fact that the tracked targets are in the viscinity of the own-vehicle, and thus small differences in geodetical coordinates can be linearly mapped to ENU differences and the other way around. x [src:"Wikipedia:Geographic coordinate conversion"]
By differentiating the model mentioned in the Coordinate systems section, one can confirm that for given own-ship geodetic coordinates $(\phi,\lambda)_{\textit{own-ship}}$
\[ \begin{bmatrix} dE\\dN\\dU \end{bmatrix}= \begin{bmatrix} (N(\phi)+h)\cos\phi &0 &0\\ 0 &M(\phi) + h &0\\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} d\lambda\\d\phi\\dh \end{bmatrix} \]
Which can be solved for
\[ \begin{bmatrix} d\lambda\\ d\phi \end{bmatrix}= \mathbf{T}(\phi)^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix} \]
Since the target's state is in the sea-keeping frame, one needs to transform it to ENU.
If $\mathbf{R}_z(h)$ is the Euler's angle transformation for a rotation $h$ around the z-axis, then the transformation from sea-keeping to ENU
\[\mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} \]
given the own-ship's seakeeping heading from true-north $h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}$ is ,
\[\mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} = \mathbf{R_{z}}(\frac{\pi}{2}-h_{0_{\textit{TN}}})\]
\[ \begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix}= \mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}} \begin{bmatrix} X\\ Y \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target wrt}_{{\textit{sea-keeping}}}} \]
and thus, by combining the above equations
\[ \begin{bmatrix} \phi\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target}}= \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{own-ship}} + \mathbf{T}(\phi_{0})^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} dE\\ dN \end{bmatrix} + w_{\textit{AIS}}=\]
\[ \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{own-ship}} + \mathbf{T}(\phi_{0})^{-1} \mathbf{R}^{\textit{ENU}}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}(h_{0_{\textit{TN}}}) \begin{bmatrix} X_{\textit{target}}\\ Y_{\textit{target}} \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{wrt}_{{\textit{sea-keeping}}}}+w_{\textit{AIS}} \]
Where
 \(\mathbf{T}(\phi)= \begin{bmatrix} N(\phi)\cos\phi &0\\ 0 &M(\phi) \end{bmatrix} \) is the transformation matrix from geodetic differences to ENU differences.
 \(N(\phi) = \frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{1-e^2\sin^2\phi}}\) is the prime vertical radius of curvature
 \(M(\phi) = \frac{\alpha(1-e^2)}{(1-e^2\sin^2\phi)^\frac{3}{2}}\) is the meridional radius of curvature
 \(\alpha,\beta \) are the equitorial radius(6378.1370 km) and the Polar radius(6356.7523 km) respectively, as derived from the WGS-84 ellipsoid model.
 \(e = \sqrt{1 -\frac{b^2}{a^2}}\) is the ellipsoid's eccentricity.
 \(h = 0 \) since the own-ship is always floating on the geoid.
 \(w_{\textit{AIS}}\) is white gaussian zero-mean noise with covariance matrix \(\mathbf{R}_{\textit{AIS}}\) depending on the noise intensity of the AIS source.
  The above equation can be rewritten to the standard measurement model formulation
\[ z_{AIS}(\mathbf{x},\begin{bmatrix}\phi_{0}, \lambda_{0}, h_{0_{\textit{TN}}} \end{bmatrix}^{T}) = \begin{bmatrix} \phi\\ \lambda \end{bmatrix}_{\textit{target}} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{0}\\ \lambda_{0} \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} + w_{\textit{AIS}} \]
, which is a linear measurement model that is approximating the geoid with a spherical-linearization about the own-ship's geodetic location.

Camera model Assuming a camera sensor and a pinhole model, with the camera z-axis aligned with the own-ship body frame
   Camera alignment with body frame   
A top-view of the pinhole model seen in the figure below
   Pinhole model top-view   
Then the measurement model for the predicted pixel location of a target obtained from a deep neural-network classifier and localiser along the y-axis is found by the projecting a point in homogeneous coordinates
\[x_{\text{wrt}_{\text{sea keeping}}} =\begin{bmatrix}x, y, 0, 1\end{bmatrix}^T\]
to the finite camera frame given measured information $z_{gyro}$ about the rotational state of the own-ship w.r.t the sea-keeping frame:
\[ z_{\textit{cam}_{\textit{px}}}(\mathbf{x},z_{\textit{gyro}}) = \frac{\begin{bmatrix}-P_1-\\-P_2-\end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}}{ \begin{bmatrix} -P_3- \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}}+ w_{\text{camera}} \]
Where $\mathbf{P}$ is the projection matrix and $P_{i}$ it's rows
\[ \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix}-P_1- \\-P_2-\\-P_3- \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K}\mathbf{\mathbf{R}_{\textit{own-ship}}^{\textit{camera}}}[\mathbf{I} | \mathbf{-C}] \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{\mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}} &\mathbf{0}\\ \mathbf{0} &1 \end{bmatrix}\]
Because of the denominator this is a non-linear model, and a description of the matrices that comprise the projection matrix \(P\) is given below:
\(\mathbf{K}\) is the intrinsic camera calibration matrix
\[\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}f m_x &0 &p_x \\ 0 &f m_y &p_y \\ 0 &0 &1 \end{bmatrix} \]
with
 \(f\) is the intrinsic camera calibration parameter, focal length in mm \(m_x, m_y\) are the camera's sensor pixel density in \(\frac{\text{number of pixels}}{mm}\) across the two different directions of the image plane. \(p_x,p_y\) correspond to the position of the principal point of the image in pixel coordinates \(w_{\textit{camera}}\) is white gaussian zero mean noise with variance related to the classifier accuracy, or any calibration errors in the extrinsic and intrinsic camera matrices.  $\mathbf{R}_{\textit{own-ship}}^{\textit{camera}}$ is the extrinsic camera rotation matrix that describes the orientation of the camera frame w.r.t. the own-ship.
$\mathbf{C}$ is the extrinsic origin of the camera frame w.r.t. the own-ship.
$\mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}$ Is the transformation matrix that corresponds to the roll-pitch rotation of the own-ship w.r.t the sea-keeping frame. Please note that the order of the rotations matters.
\[ \mathbf{R}_{\textit{sea-keeping}}^{\textit{own-ship}}=\left( R_{\textit{pitch}} R_{\textit{roll}}\right)^T \]
Where \(R_{\textit{pitch}},R_{\textit{roll}}\) are the standard rotation matrices for Euler's angles $z_{\textit{gyro}}=(\alpha,\beta)^T = (\textit{roll},\textit{pitch})^T$.
\[ R_{\textit{roll}}= R_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 &0 &0\\ 0 &\cos\alpha &-\sin \alpha\\ 0 &\sin \alpha &\cos \alpha \end{bmatrix} \]
and
\[ R_{\textit{pitch}}= R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta &0 &\sin \beta\\ 0 &1 &0\\ -\sin \beta &0 &\cos\beta \end{bmatrix} \]
A basic assumption at that point, is that the own-ship is able to measure the angles $(\alpha,\beta)$ of the own-ship body frame w.r.t the sea-keeping frame. These angles are usually available on a ship by using one or multiple gyroscope sensors.
Kalman filter Modeling Assumptions:  System's state evolution is linear and driven by  Known input Additive process noise which is assumed to be a zero-mean white, uncorrelated process with known covariance \(Q(k)\)  Measurements - linear sensor function of the state with  Addditive measurement zero-mean white noise with known covariance $R(k)$  Initial state , a random variable with known mean(initial estimate) and covariance (initial uncertainty)     Block diagram of a discrete Kalman filtering algorithm   
src
Under the above assumptions the Kalman Filter is the MMSE state estimator. If the random variables are not Gaussian, but one can still obtain their first two moments, then the KF is still the best linear MMSE estimator.
KF pitfalls in tracking  Usually the motion models are nonlinear The measurement models are usually nonlinear Unable to capture mode changes(target maneuveres) Can't handle cross-correlated noise Single target & unimodal  Extended Kalman Filter The EKF is a suboptimal state estimation algorithm for nonlinear systems.
Modeling Assumptions:  Same as the KF except that the motion model and/or the measurement models are nonlinear functions of the state, note that the noises are still assumed to enter additively into the models. First order Taylor expansion of the nonlinear functions are a reasonable approximation to the original functions  Taylor approximation of the CTRV motion models A nonlinear motion model $x_{k} = f({x}{k-1})$ can be linearised around a known state $x{k-1}$
\[ f_{k-1} \approx F_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \nabla_{x_{k-1}} f_{k-1}^{T}(x_{k-1}) \end{bmatrix}_{x_{k-1} \rightarrow\text{latest estimate}}^{T} \]
and for the CTRV model this leads to
\[ F_{{CTRV}_{k-1}} = \begin{bmatrix}1 &0 &\frac{sin(h+T\omega) - \sinh}{\omega} & \upsilon \frac{cos(h+T\omega)-cosh}{\omega} & \upsilon\frac{sinh -sin(h+T\omega) + T\omega cos(h+T\omega)}{\omega^{2}}\\ 0 &1 &-\frac{cos(h+T\omega) - cos(\omega)}{\omega} &\upsilon \frac{sin(h+T\omega)-sinh}{\omega} & \upsilon\frac{cos(h + T\omega) -cosh + T\omega sin(h+T\omega)}{\omega^{2}}\\ 0 &0 &1 &0 &0 \\ 0 &0 &0 &0 &1\end{bmatrix} \]
similarly, a measurement model $z_k=h(\mathbf{xk})$ can be linearised around the latest motion propagated estimate $x{k}$ as
\[ h_{k} \approx H_{k} = \begin{bmatrix} \nabla_{x_{k}} h_{k}^{T}(x_{k}) \end{bmatrix}_{x_{k} \rightarrow\text{motion updated estimate}}^{T} \]
Thus the radar model that measures distance and bearing(given a CTRV state-space) is linearised as
\[ H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} &\frac{y}{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}} &0 &0 &0 \\ -\frac{y}{x^2+y^2} &\frac{x}{x^2+y^2} &0 &0 &0 \end{bmatrix} \]
   Gaussian PDF propagation through a nonlinear model and taylor-approximation.   
Sebastian Thrun, Wolfram Burgard, and Dieter Fox. 2005. Probabilistic Robotics (Intelligent Robotics and Autonomous Agents). The MIT Press.
Simulation results  Motion model CTRV, sample time T = 100ms Measurement models, asynchronous measurements from:  Noisy Radar measurements: bearing and distance, Ts=5 s Noisy Camera pixel measurements. Horizontal target pixel location on the image plane. Ts = 20s      Overall estimated trajectory of target using CTRV motion model for 8.3 minutes   
Having a close look at a segment of the trajectory one can observe the effect of using a CTRV model.
 Clearly the estimator is able to adapt to the step change in the turn rate but it is rather slow, as the model is supposed to be a constant turn rate one. The red ellipses correspond to the state uncertainty as propagated through the motion model, without an available measurement update. The green ellipses correspond to the uncertainty after incorporating a measurement update.     Close up on the previous trajectory   
Filter performance Performance graphs The performance of this tracker is outlined in the following figures    Performance of the EKF on the previous simulation (right click image open in new tab for zoomable image)   
From top to bottom:
 Predicted target position $x_p$ and real target position \(x\) w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame Predicted target position $y_p$ and real target position $y$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame Predicted target heading velocity $\upsilon_p$ and real target heading velocity $\upsilon$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame Predicted target heading velocity $h_p$ and real target heading velocity $h$ w.r.t Seakeeping own-ship frame Predicted target turn rate $\omega_{p}$ and real target turn rate $\omega$ Absolute distance prediction error norm $|e| = \sqrt{(x-x_p)2+(y-y_p)2}$ Innovation norm $|v|$, is a measure of how accurate the filter is tracking the real target, an unbound innovation norm implies that the observations do not match the predicted trajectory anymore i.e the tracker diverged. Innovation of the range measurements Innovation of the range rate Measurements Innovation of the bearing measurement Innovation of the camera localiser measurements  Determinant of the covariance matrix P Calculating the determinant $||P||$ of the predicted state covariance matrix provides an indication of the tracking uncertainty of the target or in other words a measure of the surface of the plotted uncertainty ellipses. As seen in the figure below, measurement updates from different sensors are able to keep the uncertainty low.

Driving scene image segmentation using Fully Convolutional Networks Motivation Self-driving cars are one of the hottest areas of research and business for the tech giants. What seemed like a science-fiction, a few years ago, now seems more like something which is soon to become a part and parcel of life. “soon to be” is because of the fact that even though companies like Tesla, Nissan, Cadillac do have self-driving car assistance software. They still require a human to keep an eye on the road and take control when needed. However, it is fascinating to see how far we have come in terms of innovation and how fast technology is advancing.
Other applications of semantic segmentation include and definetely not limited to:
 Classification of terrain visible in satellite imagery Medical imaging analysis Industrial inspection  Background Fully Convolutional Networks 1 provide a solution to the image segmentation task, which is trying to answer to the question of ’where’ is something instead of just ’what’. The FCN complements the CNN architecture by following up the ”downsampling path” with an ”upsampling path”. Upsampling methods can include both learnable and non-learnable parameters.
   FCN8s proposed architecture    FCN extends the classic convolutional network pipeline such such that it can be used for full image predictions. The input to an FCN is an RGB image and its output is a pixelwise classification prediction. The initial layers of the pipeline are similar to a standard CNN architecture where the image is transformed into a latent representation that includes contextual information but most structural information is lost due to the nature of convolution.
  The use of CNN layers in the vanilla FCN architecture is reducing the structural information in the image as data flows through the ”downsampling path”, hence upsampling the low-dimensional information in the ”upsampling path” creates coarse bounding areas around the objects.
  The proposed architecture is leveraging the available structural information of the convolutional feature maps by forward passing information from their max-pooling layers to the up-sampling layers. Merging feature maps from various resolution levels helps combining context information with spatial information. 􏰀* The proposed architecture is leveraging transfer learning by initializing the convolutional layers from an accurate pre-trained classification architecture(VGGnet).
  The convolutional layers can be frozen or trainable. Performance of the architecture has been evaluated for both cases.
  The architecture enables end-to-end training of the network. We define different models by adding forward passing feature maps from various resolution levels.
 FCN32s : The original FCN without max-pooling forward passes. FCN16s : Forward passing max pooled information from conv4 to the output of upsample6. FCN8s : Extends FCN16s by upsampling the FCN16s skip connection, then fusing it with the max-pooled output of conv3 and applying upsample8. FCNs : Extends FCN8s by summing the pooled output of conv2 with the FCN8s skip connection(not illustrated in Figure 1). Figure 1 illustrates the FCN8s architecture, the FCN16s is derived by cutting the skip2, and FCN32s by cutting both skip1 and skip2.    Structural information is being extracted from the pooling layers of the down- sampling path. Then this information is being fused with the up-sampled information by element-wise addition before moving upstream. Batch normalization and dropout layers are being applied to the outputs of every convolution and up-sampling layer. ReLu was used as the activation function.
Dataset: Camvid and Cityscape are free available labeled dataset.
 Cambridge-driving Labeled Video Database (CamVid) is a collection of video and images where each pixel is labelled with one of the 32 semantic labels. This dataset contains both fix-perspective videos (CCTV footage) and from a driving automobile. The footage from automobile makes this dataset a suitable choice for this project.     CamVid sample image    Cityscape dataset contains labelled video and images from fix-perspective videos in the streets of different cities. The images are labelled with one of the 32 semantic labels. For the project, we would focus on the CamVid dataset recorded from automobile. Fix-perspective videos will not be investigated.     Cityscapes ground truth sample image (Zurich)    Training: Different submodels of the original architecture were tested depending on the resolution of the information extracted from the convolutional layers. Namely FCN32s, FCN16s, FCN8s.
Loss and performance metrics For the task of image segmentation pixel wise binary cross-entropy loss is used. It examines each pixels individually. Since the target vectors are one out of K encoded (total 32 class labels), the binary cross entropy is calculated using each encoded class and then averaged over all the 32 classes. This makes sure that each pixel is learning equally while training.
To compare the performance of the FCN, we will consider two measures : pixel accuracy and IoU (Intersection over Union)
$$\textrm{Pixel accuracy} = \frac{\textrm{No of pixels predicted accurately}}{\textrm{No of pixels}}$$
$$\textrm{IoU} = \frac{\textrm{Area of Overlap}}{\textrm{Area of Union}}$$
   From left to right model performance for, FCN32, FCN16, FCN8, Ground truth       Best model (FCN8s) on average Intersection over Union(IoU) performance       Models pixel accuracy over epochs. (mean epoch time 90s on a single GPU setup)    Model predictions    Different model predictions.      arXiv:1411.4038v2 [cs.CV] 8 Mar 2015 Fully Convolutional Networks for Semantic Segmentation. ↩︎
summary: Deep Fully Convolutional Networks for pixel-wise class prediction

Occupancy grid mapping in MATLAB2018a Test-case of occupancy grid mapping using real pose data, and laser measurements.
  Occupancy grid mapping with known poses.   -- Getting Started For a showcase you can copy the whole rep and run the script
occupancy_grid_mapping.m  The script will run by using the measurement file laser_0.log which includes laser sensor data as well as odometry data.
Description Odometry pose data is treated as the real pose of the robot, in that sense there is no variance/uncertainty in the pose data, hence the robot belief distribution at every step is represented by a dirac centered at the robots pose. This assumption should make sense, since we are addressing exclusively a mapping problem which requires good knowledge or the robots position to create an accurate map. By relaxing this requirement, the problem gets transformed into a SLAM problem, which is beyond the scope of this example. Another assumption is that the environment is static, thus it can get only more certain(brighter), and not more uncertain(darker) that in the initialisation.
What is occupancy grid mapping Occupancy grid mapping is a probabilistic representation of an environment. The environment is discretised into (here even) cells and the grid values represent obstacle uncertainty. In the example above, the darker the grid the more uncertain the representation. As the robot moves around getting laser range measurements, it “sheds light” into the map and brightens the free-space areas. This is done by ray-casting the measurements on the grid and decreasing the associated cell’s probability. This is a rough explanation omitting mathematical details. For a detailed and mathematically involved explanation please refer to a very nice book.
summary: Occupancy grid mapping in MATLAB2018a

Motivation Mobile robots need to be aware of moving targets in their environment, let alone they have to do it fast and using the least resources possible. The case of particular interests is the one of mobile robots operating in a factory environment, in which they have to constantly avoid other dangerous moving targets such as forklifts. The motivation for this algorithm was a company that wanted to implement a computationaly light solution since their main processing unit was already busy with running other computationally expensive tasks.
Constraints Low computational efforts exclude grid-based method, because they are having a difficulty scaling to larger areas while keeping computations low and therefore, a continuous method had to be used. Inspired from the probabilistic estimation methods the algorithm can be summarized to the following steps.
Algorithm overview  Mark polygon area as free space from t-1 scan measurement. Shrink polygon area according to sensor noise model in order to avoid false positives on step#3. Find scan points from the last scan t that are violating the free space from step#2 Assign gaussian measurement model to each violation. Calculate overall probability distribution function. Filter out violations with a lower probability than a threshold. Calculate new PDF, given the confirmed violations. Cluster confirmed violating points to separate targets. Calculate expected value of every cluster as the moving targets location and assign it to a moving target object. Propagate possible previous moving target objects based on confirmed violations field. Repeat #1 for new scan sample.    Detector animation in a structured environment with a walking target.   -- Remarks  The advantage of this method is that is effectively capitalizing the low dimensionality of the laser data, to create a fast and robust estimator, in constrast to using camera based methods which are prone to classification errors/illumination noise. It is worth noting that the “free-space” algorithm will not work for objects moving away from the laser frame, which is not necessarily a disadvantage, since generally objects moving away from the robot are not a safety concern. Although the method is robust, it is generally noisy, due to the nature of violating points. By including a dynamical model of the moving targets, one could include an optimal estimator in the pipeline that smooths out the estimated position of the targets.  Next steps
summary: Tracking moving objects using laser data
title: Moving object detector

Design and construction of FSAE car aerodynamic elements. Use of computational fluid dynamics and goal driven algorithms to optimize the geometries and setups. Initial geometry was designed in Solidworks , then a parametric CAD file was created in ANSYS. Spallart-Allmaras and K-epsilon turbulence models were used for the solver. The optimization algorithm altered variables such as absolute and relative elements pitch and relative and absolute element location and scaling, and tried to maximize the lift to drag ratio. The solver used was ANSYS-Fluent

The team’s department is the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering of Aristotle University and its members are undergraduate students from various scientific fields. Working under the pressure of demanding deadlines, the team’s members broaden their academic knowledge, theoretically and practically and gain great experience.
Aristurtle’s goal is to design and build electric race cars in order to compete in the international Formula Student competitions against universities from all over the world. Preparing towards this goal its members work in a fully structured professional environment. The team’s vision is to contribute to the upgrading of new technologies in the electric automotive field, in Greece and worldwide, by researching and getting in touch with high-level innovative products and pioneering ideas.

The capabilities of flow control using DBD were studied with a vision of being implemented in UAVs to alter flight characteristics such as power efficiency, lift off velocity, and provide abilities such as direction control without the use of moving surfaces.
    HCUAV is a research project, whose aim was to design and build a Medium-Altitude-Long-Endurance Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (MALE UAV) in order to carry out civil operations in Greece. The consortium was made up of three Greek universities and three Greek industries, and the chief Engineer was prof. K. Yakinthos (AUTH). Starting from scratch, the HCUAV prototype (RX-1) made its first and successful flight, 36 months after the initiation of the project. The maiden flight took place at August 4, 2016, and lasted approximately 15 minutes. The attached video is an overview of the testing phase, which started right after the conclusion of the aerial vehicle construction and integration.
